UserProfile.title: UserProfile
UserProfile.description: Represents a user's profile information within the AXI Shorts application.

id.description: Unique identifier for the UserProfile entity.

email.description: The user's email address, primarily for display and identification within the profile (not for authentication system data).

Video.title: Video
Video.description: Represents a short video uploaded by a user, including its metadata and cloud storage reference.

id.description: Unique identifier for the Video entity.

videoUrl.description: The URL pointing to the video file stored in cloud storage (e.g., Firebase Storage). This URL can be used for streaming and downloading the video.

uploaderId.description: Reference to the UserProfile who uploaded this video. (Relationship: UserProfile 1:N Video)

uploadTimestamp.description: Timestamp indicating when the video was uploaded.

title.description: The title of the video, potentially generated or suggested by the AI Video Caption Tool.

hashtags.description: An array of hashtags associated with the video, potentially generated or suggested by the AI Video Caption Tool.

likesCount.description: The current number of likes for the video. For MVP, this might represent an initial value.

definition.description: Stores individual user profile data, identified by the user's authentication UID. Access is restricted to the owner (`userId` must match `request.auth.uid`). This path uses direct path-based ownership for authorization independence.

params.name: userId
params.description: The unique identifier of the user, matching their Firebase Authentication UID.

definition.description: Stores short video metadata and cloud storage references. Videos are publicly readable for the dynamic feed. Write/delete access is restricted to the `uploaderId` field, which is denormalized into the document for authorization independence. This supports the dynamic video feed (QAP) and allows efficient ownership checks.

params.name: videoId
params.description: The unique identifier for the video.

firestore.reasoning:
The proposed Firestore data structure for AXI Shorts prioritizes Authorization Independence and Query-Agnostic Permissions (QAPs) as core design principles. We will use two top-level collections:

1.  "/users/{userId}": This collection will store individual UserProfile documents. Each document's ID will be the user's Firebase Authentication UID. This directly implements the 'Private Data' access model and 'Hierarchical Paths for User-Owned Data' strategy. Access rules will be straightforward: a user can only read/write their own profile by matching `request.auth.uid` to the document's `userId`. This ensures **Authorization Independence** for user profiles, as no `get()` calls are needed to verify ownership.

2.  "/videos/{videoId}": This collection will store all Video metadata. Each Video document includes an `uploaderId` field, which is a direct reference to the Firebase Authentication UID of the user who uploaded the video. This field is a crucial example of denormalization for authorization: it copies the relevant authorization context (who owns/uploaded this video) directly into the video document itself. This is critical for **Authorization Independence** because Firestore security rules can check `resource.data.uploaderId == request.auth.uid` for write/delete operations without needing to perform a `get()` operation on a parent user document or any other external document. All videos in this collection share a homogeneous security posture: everyone can read (for the dynamic feed), but only the `uploaderId` can modify or delete their own video.

This structure strongly supports **QAPs** (Query-Agnostic Permissions). For the dynamic video feed, a simple query on the `/videos` collection (e.g., `db.collection('videos').orderBy('uploadTimestamp', 'desc')`) will work efficiently because the entire collection is readable by everyone. There's no need for complex filtering or rules to restrict visible documents based on user roles or ownership during a global feed query. For user-specific 'My Videos' views, a query like `db.collection('videos').where('uploaderId', '==', request.auth.uid)` is equally efficient and secure due to the denormalized `uploaderId` field and the clear read/write rules.
